feedback.text: The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.

sections.title: Butternut Box's Submission to the European Commission's Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
path: Butternut Box's Submission to the European Commission's Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act

section_path: Butternut Box's Submission to the European Commission's Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: We are writing in response to the 'Digital Fairness Fitness Check' (the Consultation) and the proposed 'Digital Fairness Act' (DFA), which aims, among other things, to address manipulative and unfair practices that exploit consumers in the digital environment, such as dark patterns, addictive designs, unfair pricing practices and opaque digital contracts. We are committed to clarity and fairness, and our customers are at the heart of everything we do. We are therefore supportive of legislative progress which advances consumer rights. As a European business operating primarily via subscriptions and growing rapidly we feel our success and the satisfaction of our customers is testament to the fact that there is a need for subscription-based business models which provide customers with certainty, convenience and peace of mind. Within this letter we hope to outline how some of the proposals of the DFA may negatively impact consumer choice, convenience and enjoyment of subscriptions.

section_path: Butternut Box's Submission to the European Commission's Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: We are Butternut Box, a fresh pet food manufacturer and retailer currently serving over 300,000 dogs in the UK, Ireland, Germany, Poland, the Netherlands, Belgium, and the Czech Republic with ambitious expansion plans. We have two kitchens where we prepare, cook, freeze, store and pack our meals: one in Doncaster, England and the other our newly opened facility in Zabrze, Poland. Our suppliers are based across Europe and we work with a network of fulfilment and delivery companies to pack, box and deliver our meals to our customers. Our meals are freshly prepared and therefore perishable.

section_path: Butternut Box's Submission to the European Commission's Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: We operate a subscription-based model, selling our fresh pet food from our e-shops direct-to-consumer. The vast majority of our customers sign up online, where they create an account and sign up to an ongoing subscription. Customer boxes are delivered on a regular cadence (fortnightly / monthly / 6-weekly), with the majority of our customer base receiving a box every 3-4 weeks. By setting up an account, customers have full control over their subscription, including the ability to amend or stop their subscription at any time. We are also available 7 days a week, should a customer require or prefer assistance to amend or stop their subscription. We hold an average of 4.8 satisfaction rating on Trustpilot and Google, and have held this rating or higher for the past 8-9 years of trading, all of which time we were operating a subscription-based model, demonstrating that customers enjoy, value and appreciate the repeat service we provide to them and their pet.

section_path: Butternut Box's Submission to the European Commission's Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: We appreciate that the Consultation has emphasised that a 'one-size-fits-all' approach to regulating subscriptions would not be appropriate. We support this, since there are clear distinctions between subscriptions for regularly delivered physical (and perishable) products, and other subscriptions such as digital-only subscriptions which may be lower interaction with longer renewal periods and upfront annual costs.

section_path: Butternut Box's Submission to the European Commission's Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Deliveries of physical goods reduce the risk of unintentional subscriptions that can be seen with digital subscriptions, as there is a transparent and frequent interaction between company and customer in the form of a box of perishable goods arriving on the doorstep. Customers do not often forget perishable goods subscriptions which are factored into regular household spend, and for our customers our deliveries form a large portion, if not all, of their pets' food intake for a defined period.

section_path: Butternut Box's Submission to the European Commission's Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: A clear distinction should be made between auto-renewal reminders for longer term, lower interaction subscriptions and delivery reminders from companies delivering physical products on a subscription basis. Customers of the latter do not expect or need to receive reminders on the lead times that they would for the former; in fact given the frequency of deliveries if we were to extend lead times on delivery reminders we would risk customer confusion on delivery dates and deadlines to make changes to their boxes, and would risk our valuable delivery emails becoming seen as 'spam'. With our business model delivery reminders are not valuable unless they are within a matter of days of the delivery.

section_path: Butternut Box's Submission to the European Commission's Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: We are supportive of the consultation's approach to regulating loyalty traps. We are highly transparent through our customer journey about the nature of our proposition being a subscription, including at check-out, by sending delivery reminders, and showing the 'next delivery date' within a customer's account. However, providing a lower cost entry plan to trial a product is important for customers, in particular for our business where the ultimate consumer is a pet who in a lot of cases is transitioning from other categories of pet food. Customers should not be forced to commit to a higher entry price point, while they are quite literally trialling food with their pet.

section_path: Butternut Box's Submission to the European Commission's Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Requiring subscriptions such as ours to adopt 'active opt-in' processes would create a large administrative burden for customers. Our customers would be required to do this on a very frequent (mostly monthly) basis, which we do not believe is the intention of the DFA. Requiring an active step from customers before receipt of each box would negate the value proposition of the high frequency perishable goods subscription: customers want to be able to feed their pets high-quality meals without the administrative burden of repeatedly sourcing or cooking these meals. If customers did not opt-in in time, this would not only create operational challenges for us as regards packing and distribution timelines, but it would also risk customers being left without their pet's food. An opt-in would further disadvantage the portion of our customers who only interact with their subscription by contacting us on the phone. Lastly, we go above and beyond as a business to ensure that pets fed via our subscriptions are not left without food due to administrative or human error: in most markets if a payment bounces we will still send a box and seek to recoup the cost at

section_path: Butternut Box's Submission to the European Commission's Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: a later date. We do this because customers rely on these deliveries on the cadence they choose and we are mindful of the trust that has been placed in us to deliver. Requiring an active opt-in would simply not suit our customers or how we aim to serve them.

section_path: Butternut Box's Submission to the European Commission's Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: We are grateful for the opportunity to contribute to the European Commission's Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act.

section_path: Butternut Box's Submission to the European Commission's Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act
